edges.description: Loss of lower motor neurons denervates skeletal muscle, producing weakness and wasting.

nodes.description: Progressive degeneration of lower motor neurons in the anterior (ventral) horn of the spinal cord and the brainstem motor nuclei produces denervation of skeletal muscle. Loss of these alpha motor neurons clinically manifests as flaccid weakness, muscle atrophy, fasciculations, and hyporeflexia or areflexia, with no clinical evidence of upper motor neuron involvement at presentation.

evidence.snippet: Progressive muscular atrophy (PMA) is clinically characterized by signs of lower motor neuron dysfunction and may evolve into amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
All patients showed significant TDP-43 linked degeneration of LMNs

locations: anterior horn of the spinal cord

nodes.description: Loss of lower motor neurons removes trophic and contractile innervation of skeletal muscle fibers, producing chronic neurogenic atrophy. Clinically this manifests as relentlessly progressive flaccid weakness and wasting, often beginning asymmetrically and distally, accompanied by fasciculations and cramps, with electrophysiological evidence of denervation. Progressive respiratory muscle involvement (declining vital capacity) is the principal determinant of survival.

evidence.snippet: Vital capacity (VC) at baseline and decrease of VC during the first 6 months were significantly associated with outcome.
